hero.title: History of the Harkis
hero.subtitle: A visual and documentary journey through commitment, abandonment, exile, and the transmission of Harki memory.

emptyState.title: History coming soon
emptyState.body: Historical content will be available soon. In the meantime, explore our other sections.

cta.title: Let us preserve this memory together
cta.body: Join our community to share testimonies and contribute to our collective memory.

chapters.title: Origins: the commitment of the Harkis
chapters.content:
In 1954, as the Algerian War began, many Muslim Algerians chose to join the French side. These men, known as Harkis (from the Arabic word harka, meaning movement), were recruited as auxiliaries of the French army. They served in special units known as harkas and took part in operations aimed at maintaining order and pacification.

Their commitment was driven by a range of motives: loyalty to France, opposition to the FLN, the hope of a French Algeria, or simply economic necessity. Often from rural backgrounds, they saw this commitment as a way to serve and protect their families.

Between 1954 and 1962, roughly 200,000 to 250,000 Harkis served in the ranks of the French army, forming an essential force in the fight against the nationalist insurgency.

media.alt: Young Harki photographed in Algeria during the Algerian War
media.caption: A young Harki photographed in Algeria during the Algerian War.

chapters.title: Escalation: in the heart of the conflict
chapters.content:
From 1957 onward, the conflict intensified. The Harkis became increasingly involved in military operations. They knew the land, the language, and local customs, which made them invaluable auxiliaries for the French army.

They took part in major operations, sweeps in the djebels, border surveillance, and intelligence gathering. Their role became crucial in the fight against FLN guerrilla units.

This period was also marked by escalating violence. The Harkis and their families became prime targets for the FLN, which viewed them as traitors. Despite these risks, many continued to serve with courage, often at the cost of their own lives and those of their relatives.

media.alt: Portrait of a Harki veteran of World War II during the Algerian War
media.caption: Portrait of a Harki veteran of World War II during the Algerian War.

chapters.title: Abandonment: the Évian Accords and their consequences
chapters.content:
On March 18, 1962, the Évian Accords brought the Algerian War to an end. Algeria's independence was proclaimed, but the accords offered no protection for the Harkis and their families.

In this context, the French army was ordered not to repatriate the Harkis. Only a few thousand managed to leave Algeria clandestinely or with the help of officers who disobeyed orders.

For the vast majority of Harkis, this meant abandonment. Disarmed and left on their own in an independent Algeria, they were exposed to immediate reprisals. Between April and September 1962, tens of thousands were massacred. Estimates range from 30,000 to 150,000 deaths.

media.alt: FLN delegation during the Évian Accords negotiations in 1962
media.caption: The FLN delegation during the Évian Accords negotiations in 1962.
media.focus: End of war and political turning point

chapters.title: Exile: transit and housing camps
chapters.content:
The Harkis who managed to leave Algeria arrived in France under dramatic conditions. They were first confined in makeshift transit camps, then in housing camps such as Rivesaltes, Bias, and Saint-Maurice-l'Ardoise.

These camps, often located in former military sites or precarious barracks, housed entire families in extremely difficult conditions. Isolation, overcrowding, poor hygiene, and the absence of privacy deeply marked this generation.

Many families remained there for years. Children grew up in these places, facing exclusion and discrimination. Even so, the Harkis tried to rebuild their lives, taking on the hardest jobs to support their loved ones.

media.alt: Entrance to the Rivesaltes camp and memorial
media.caption: Entrance to the Rivesaltes camp and memorial, a central site in the memory of exile.

media.alt: Former dwelling in the Montmeyan forestry hamlet
media.caption: A former dwelling in the Montmeyan forestry hamlet, another long-term form of settlement in France.

chapters.title: Recognition: toward official memory
chapters.content:
From the 1970s onward, the situation slowly began to change. Children of Harkis born in France organized themselves and demanded recognition for their parents' history.

In 1975, an initial law was passed to improve the condition of the Harkis, but it remained insufficient. Associations multiplied to defend their rights and make their history known.

The 1980s and 1990s saw a gradual awakening. Historians began writing about this forgotten chapter of history. The media also became more interested in a community that had long been pushed into silence.

In 1994, a law officially recognized the Harkis as repatriates of North African origin. Some measures were introduced, but much remained to be done.

media.caption: A veteran card related to the Harkis, a concrete symbol of recognition that came late and remained partial.

chapters.title: Official recognition: from memory to history
chapters.content:
On September 25, 2001, President Jacques Chirac officially acknowledged France's debt of honor to the Harkis. This statement marked a historical turning point.

In 2005, a compensation fund was created for the Harkis and their descendants. Social and professional support measures were also strengthened.

On April 14, 2012, President Nicolas Sarkozy acknowledged France's responsibility in abandoning the Harkis. This long-awaited statement carried great symbolic weight for the whole community.

At the same time, commemorative monuments were erected and the national day of homage on September 25 became firmly established in public life.

media.alt: Homage ceremony for the Harkis in Toulon in 2006
media.caption: A ceremony honoring the Harkis in Toulon, illustrating their gradual inclusion in official commemorations.
media.sourceLabel: Homage ceremony for the Harkis, Toulon, 2006

media.caption: The Harkis memorial stele in Saint-Pons-de-Thomières shows how this memory has entered public space.

chapters.title: Transmission: preserving memory for the future
chapters.content:
Today, the community of Harkis and their descendants numbers several hundred thousand people in France. The third and fourth generations carry this memory and continue the struggle for recognition and transmission.

Associations such as AHEDNA play an essential role in preserving this memory. They organize commemorations, conferences, exhibitions, and moments of exchange to make this history known to younger generations.

The contemporary challenge is twofold: transmitting this history within families and fully anchoring it in the national narrative. It is about making this chapter of history known, understanding the suffering it involved, and honoring the courage and loyalty of the Harkis.

The future depends on education, memory, and recognition. Descendants of the Harkis are fully French while also carrying a history that is an integral part of the history of France.

media.alt: National Harkis tribute day in Reims
media.caption: National Harkis tribute day in Reims, reflecting the public transmission work carried out today.

media.alt: Poster for the Treize chibanis harkis exhibition at the Rivesaltes memorial
media.caption: Poster for the 'Treize chibanis harkis' exhibition at the Rivesaltes memorial, a sign of today's transmission work.
media.sourceLabel: 'Treize chibanis harkis' exhibition at the Rivesaltes memorial

about.title: Transmit, recognize, and bring people together around Harki memory
about.body1: AHEDNA, the Association of Harkis and Their Children of Dordogne and Nouvelle-Aquitaine, keeps the history of the Harkis and their families alive through acts of remembrance, transmission, and solidarity.
about.body2: We connect family stories, archives, commemorations, and association gatherings to make this memory visible, accessible, and alive.
